name: Repair Cost
description: A resource or currency cost paid to restore item durability, vehicle condition, equipment charges, or base structures.
design_notes: Repair costs are economy sinks tied to use, risk, and failure recovery. They work when players can predict what caused wear, what repair restores, and whether partial repair, replacement, or upgrade is the better choice.
design_purpose: Create a recurring sink that scales with equipment value and usage.
Make durability, damage, or maintenance matter without permanently deleting player investment.
Encourage planning before long expeditions, races, or combat encounters.
player_fantasy: The player feels they are maintaining valuable gear and deciding when restoration is worth the cost.

parameters.name: repair_cost_curve_id
parameters.description: Formula or table used to convert missing durability into price.

parameters.name: durability_restore_percent
parameters.description: Portion of max durability restored by one repair transaction.

parameters.name: rarity_cost_multiplier
parameters.description: Cost modifier applied by item rarity_tier or equipment class.

parameters.name: repair_floor_minor_units
parameters.description: Minimum cost charged so tiny damage does not create free durability restoration.

parameters.name: repair_vendor_id
parameters.description: Source of repair rules, discounts, stock requirements, or location restrictions.

edge_cases: The item is equipped, traded, destroyed, or upgraded while a repair confirmation dialog is open.
Repairing above max durability due to buffs or temporary modifiers must define the cap clearly.
A repair kit uses materials while a vendor uses coins, so failure rollback must restore the correct resource type.
Server correction after combat damage can change the durability value that the client priced locally.
common_bugs: Repair cost is calculated from displayed durability percent instead of exact durability_state, causing rounding exploits.
Transaction debits currency before validating item_instance_id ownership and loses coins on rejected repair.
Partial repair updates the UI but does not persist the new durability value to save data.
Discounts apply to repair cost but not to affordability checks, so valid purchases are incorrectly disabled.
balancing_notes: Repair should be noticeable enough to make damage meaningful but not so punishing that players avoid using interesting gear.
Rarity scaling needs a ceiling; otherwise high-tier equipment can become too expensive to use in ordinary content.
Repair sinks should be tuned alongside loot replacement rates and durability loss frequency.
accessibility_notes: Show before/after durability, exact cost, and resource type before confirmation.
Warn clearly when a repair is partial or when repairing consumes rare materials instead of common currency.

implementation_notes.unity: Run repair through an item_instance_id transaction: validate ownership and durability_state, quote repair_cost_curve_id, debit currency or materials, update durability, then emit UI refresh from the committed result.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep repair logic in an inventory service rather than vendor UI nodes; pass item_instance_id and repair_vendor_id to validate ownership, cost, and restore percent before saving.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use an authoritative inventory/equipment component for repair RPCs; the server should recompute cost from replicated durability and reject stale client quotes.
implementation_notes.web: Represent repair as a transaction object with before/after durability and resource diff so failed persistence can roll back wallet and item state together.

example_games.title: The Witcher 3
example_games.note: Uses equipment durability and repair through vendors or kits.

example_games.title: World of Warcraft
example_games.note: Uses vendor repair costs for damaged equipment.
